prompt:
Before solving this problem, analyze it:
(a) What type of problem is this?
(b) What cognitive strategy is most relevant?
(c) What will the hardest part be?
Then solve it.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"problem_type": "...", "strategy": "...", "hardest_part": "...", "solution": "..."}

Problem: A software company is losing 5% of users per month (churn). The product
team proposes building 10 new features that exiting users requested
in surveys. The data team notes that users who leave typically used
only 2 of the existing 30 features, while users who stay use an
average of 8 features. What does this suggest the real problem is,
and what would be a more effective intervention than building new
features?
response: {"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"duration_ms":10271,"duration_api_ms":10222,"num_turns":1,"result":"```json\n{\"problem_type\": \"Causal reasoning / business strategy / confounded data interpretation\", \"strategy\": \"Distinguish correlation from causation; identify the true lever by examining the contrast class (stayers vs. leavers)\", \"hardest_part\": \"Resisting the intuitive but wrong inference that churning users want more features — the survey data is biased toward feature requests because that's what surveys ask about, not why users actually leave\", \"solution\": \"The real problem is activation/engagement depth, not feature breadth. Leavers use only 2 of 30 features — they never became deeply embedded in the product. This suggests the issue is onboarding failure or poor discoverability, not missing functionality. Building 10 more features makes this worse: it increases surface area without fixing the shallow adoption that precedes churn. The survey data is misleading — users who are about to leave are asked 'what features do you want?' and answer that question, but the actual reason they leave is that they never found sufficient value in what already exists. A more effective intervention: (1) Identify which 2 features leavers used vs. which 8 stayers use — find the 'sticky' features that correlate with retention; (2) Redesign onboarding to drive new users toward those high-retention features faster; (3) Add in-product prompts to surface underused high-value features to at-risk users. The insight is that 30 features exist and leavers only touched 2 — the bottleneck is adoption depth, not feature count.\"}\n```","stop_reason":"end_turn","session_id":"e0364c9f-e7a1-495d-ba92-6ead8ad3327f","total_cost_usd":0.04779435,"usage":{"input_tokens":2,"cache_creation_input_tokens":10437,"cache_read_input_tokens":11182,"output_tokens":353,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":10437},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"},"modelUsage":{"claude-sonnet-4-6":{"inputTokens":2,"outputTokens":353,"cacheReadInputTokens":11182,"cacheCreationInputTokens":10437,"webSearchRequests":0,"costUSD":0.04779435,"contextWindow":200000,"maxOutputTokens":32000}},"permission_denials":[],"terminal_reason":"completed","fast_mode_state":"off","uuid":"5746bfb4-0072-44eb-87e4-19c5cb47c5c6"}

parsed.problem_type: Causal reasoning / business strategy / confounded data interpretation
parsed.strategy: Distinguish correlation from causation; identify the true lever by examining the contrast class (stayers vs. leavers)
parsed.hardest_part: Resisting the intuitive but wrong inference that churning users want more features — the survey data is biased toward feature requests because that's what surveys ask about, not why users actually leave
parsed.solution: The real problem is activation/engagement depth, not feature breadth. Leavers use only 2 of 30 features — they never became deeply embedded in the product. This suggests the issue is onboarding failure or poor discoverability, not missing functionality. Building 10 more features makes this worse: it increases surface area without fixing the shallow adoption that precedes churn. The survey data is misleading — users who are about to leave are asked 'what features do you want?' and answer that question, but the actual reason they leave is that they never found sufficient value in what already exists. A more effective intervention: (1) Identify which 2 features leavers used vs. which 8 stayers use — find the 'sticky' features that correlate with retention; (2) Redesign onboarding to drive new users toward those high-retention features faster; (3) Add in-product prompts to surface underused high-value features to at-risk users. The insight is that 30 features exist and leavers only touched 2 — the bottleneck is adoption depth, not feature count.
